free_text: Production of neutron-rich Bi isotopes by transfer
reactions
The 88-Inch Cyclotron at the Lawrence
Berkeley Laboratory.
Si(Au) surface barrier detectors.
The chemical procedure used to
separate bismuth isotopes after irradiation was similar
for all of the targets. The targets were dissolved in
~0.5 ml warm acid containing a known quantity of
38-yr 207Bi for chemical yield determination.
Concentrated HC1 containing a few drops of 6M HNO3
was used to dissolve the Pb and Tl targets, and aqua
regia was used for the HgS targets. The
radiochemically separated Bi samples were measured
with an alpha-spectrometer system consisting of four
independent Si(Au) surface barrier detectors allowing
four samples to be counted simultaneously. Counting
of a sample was typically started 20-30 min after the
end of an irradiation and continued for a period of
several hours.
Overall errors are estimated to be
estimated <50% for all points.
BP
The lead targets were enriched
in Pb (98.7%) with thicknesses ranging from 3.4 to
4.0 mg/cm2. All the targets had thin Al
coatings of 0.4-0.7 mg/cm2 and thicker Al backings
(2.17 mg/cm2) to stop the recoiling reaction products.
Fig. 2, page 2161.

In the case of mercury, targets
of both natural isotopic composition and those
enriched in Hg (98.2%) were used. The thicknesses of
the natural targets were 0.6-0.8 mg/cm2 and those of
enriched targets 0.2-0.8 mg/cm2. In both cases the
values refer to the chemical compound HgS, the form
in which Hg was used. All the targets had thin Al
coatings of 0.4-0.7 mg/cm2 and thicker Al backings
(2.17 mg/cm2) to stop the recoiling reaction products.
Fig. 4, page 2162.
